audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a student talking to the student accommodation officer at a college.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I've just been accepted on a course at the university, and I'd like to try and arrange accommodation in the hall of residence.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, certainly.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Please sit down.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What I'll do is fill in a form with you to find out a little more about your preferences and so forth.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The student wishes to arrange accommodation in a hall of residence, so hall has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I've just been accepted on a course at the university, and I'd like to try and arrange accommodation in the hall of residence.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, certainly.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Please sit down.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What I'll do is fill in a form with you to find out a little more about your preferences and so forth.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So, first of all, um, can I take your name?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's Anu Bhatt.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Could you spell your name please?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, ANU BHATT.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Thanks, and could I ask your date of birth?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: March 31st, 1972.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And where are you from?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: India.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And um what will you be studying?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm doing a course in nursing.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right, thank you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And how long would you want to stay in hall do you think?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, it'll take 3 years.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But I'd only like to stay in hall for two.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'd like to think about living outside for the third year.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And what did you have in mind for catering?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Do you want to cook for yourself, or have all your meals provided, that's full board?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Is there something in between?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, you can just have evening meal provided, which is half board.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's what I'd prefer.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, a lot of students opt for that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Now, with that in mind, do you have any special diet, anything we should know about?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I don't take red meat.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No red meat.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, thinking about the room itself, we have a number of options.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. You can have a single study bedroom, or you can have a shared one.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: These are both what we call simple rooms.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The other alternative is to opt for a single bedsit, which actually has more space and better facilities.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. There's about £20 a week difference between them.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, actually my grant is quite generous, and I think the bedsit sounds the best option.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Lovely.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'll put you down for that, and we'll see what availability is like.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Now, can I ask some other personal details which we like to have on record?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I wonder if you could let us know what your interests are.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: This might help us get a closer match for placing you in a particular hall.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Ummm. Well, I love the theater.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And I enjoy sports, uh, particularly badminton.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Ah, that's worth knowing.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Now, what we finish with on the form is really a list from you of what your priorities are in choosing a hall, and we'll do our best to take these into account.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, the first thing is I'd prefer a hall where there are other mature students, if possible.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, we do have halls which tend to cater for slightly older students.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. And I'd prefer to be out of town.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's actually very good for you, because we tend to have more vacancies in out-of-town halls.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Ah. Lucky.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, uh, anything else?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, I would like somewhere with a shared area, a TV room for example, or something like that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's a good way to socialize.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Certainly is.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's it.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Now we just need a contact telephone number for you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, sure, I'll just find it.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Great, so we'll be in contact with you as soon as possible.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Accommodation Form - Student Information

test_text.title: Introduction to the Wildlife Club and Nearby Areas

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man giving a talk to new members of a wildlife club in the south of England.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 13.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 11 to 13.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, I'm delighted to welcome you to our wildlife club, and very pleased that you're interested in the countryside, and the plants and creatures of this area.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I think you'll be surprised at the variety we have here, even though we're not far from London.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'll start by telling you about some of the parks and open spaces nearby.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One very pleasant place is Halland Common.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This has been public land for hundreds of years, and what you'll find interesting is that the River Ouse, which flows into the sea 80 km away, has its source in the common.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's an information board about the plants and animals you can see here, and by the way, the common is accessible 24 hours a day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then there's Holt Island, which is noted for its great range of trees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the past willows were grown here commercially for basket making.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And this ancient craft has recently been reintroduced.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The island is only open to the public from Friday to Sunday, because it's quite small.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And if there were people around every day, much of the wildlife would keep away.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: From there, it's just a short walk across the bridge to Longfield Country Park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Longfield has a modern replica of a farm from over 2,000 years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Children's activities are often arranged there, like bread making and face painting.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The park is only open during daylight hours, so bear that in mind if you decide to go there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 14 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 14 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Longfield Park has a program of activities throughout the year, and to give you a sample, this is what's happening in the next few days.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On Monday, you can learn about herbs and how they've been used over the centuries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll start with a tour of our herb garden, practice the technique of using them as color dyes for cloth, and listen to an illustrated talk about their use in cooking and medicine.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then on Wednesday, you can join local experts to discover the variety of insects and birds that appear in the evening.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We keep to a small number of people in the group, so if you want to go, you'll need to phone the park ranger a few days ahead.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's a small charge which you should pay when you turn up.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'm sure you're all keen to help with the practical task of looking after the park, so on Saturday you can join a working party.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll have a choice of all sorts of activities from planting hedges to picking up litter.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So you'll be able to change from one to another when you feel like it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The rangers will be hard at work all day, but do come and join in, even for just a short while.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One thing though is to make sure you're wearing something that you don't mind getting dirty or torn.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And finally, I'd like to tell you about our new wildlife area, Hinchingbrooke Park, which will be opened to the public next month.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This slide doesn't really indicate how big it is, but anyway, you can see the two gates into the park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the main paths.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you can see, there's a lake in the northwest of the park, with a bird hide to the west of it, at the end of a path.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So it'll be a nice quiet place for watching the birds on the lake.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Fairly close to where refreshments are available, there's a dog walking area in the southern part of the park, leading off from the path.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And if you just want to sit and relax, you can go to the flower garden.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's the circular area on the map, surrounded by paths.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And finally, there's a wooded area in the western section of the park between two paths.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, that's enough from me, so let's go on to...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: As you can see, there's a lake in the northwest of the park, with a bird hide to the west of it, at the end of a path.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So it'll be a nice quiet place for watching the birds on the lake.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Fairly close to where refreshments are available, there's a dog walking area in the southern part of the park, leading off from the path.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And if you just want to sit and relax, you can go to the flower garden.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's the circular area on the map, surrounded by paths.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And finally, there's a wooded area in the western section of the park between two paths.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, that's enough from me, so let's go on to...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: reconstruction of a 2,000-year-old <input> with activities for children
body.title: Parks and open spaces

items.title: As part of Monday's activity, visitors will
For the activity on Saturday, visitors should
options: make sure they are able to stay for the whole day.
tell the rangers before the event what they wish to do.

test_text.title: Improving the Self-access Centre for English Language Students

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear the director of studies in an English language center and a student representative talking about their self-access center.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Jun.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: As you know, I've asked you here today to discuss the future of our Self-access Center.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: We have to decide what we want to do about this very important resource.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: For our English language students.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: So, can you tell me what the students think about this?
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Well, from the students' point of view, we would like to keep it.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: The majority of students say that they enjoy using it because it provides a variation on the classroom routine, and they see it as a pretty major component of their course.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: But we would like to see some improvements to the equipment, particularly the computers.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: There aren't enough for one each at the moment, and we always have to share.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: Well, yes.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: The teachers agree that it is a very valuable resource, but one thing we have noticed is that a lot of the students are using it to check their personal emails.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: We don't want to stop you students using it.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: But we think the computers should be used as a learning resource, not for emails.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Some of us also think that we could benefit a lot more by relocating the Self-access Center to the main university library building.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: How do you think the students would feel about that, Jun?
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Well, the library is big enough to incorporate the Self-access Center, but it wouldn't be like a class activity anymore.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: Our main worry would be not being able to go to a teacher for advice.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: I'm sure there would be plenty of things to do, but we really need teachers to help us choose the best activities.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: Well, there would still be a teacher present, and he or she would guide the activities of the students.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: We wouldn't just leave them to get on with it.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Yes, but I think the students would be much happier keeping the existing set-up.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: They really like going to the Self-access Center with their teacher, and staying together as a group to do activities.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: If we could just improve the resources and facilities, I think it would be fine.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: Is the cost going to be a problem?
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: It's not so much the expense that I'm worried about.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: And we've certainly got room to do it, but it's the problem of timetabling a teacher to be in there outside class hours.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: If we're going to spend a lot of money on equipment and resources.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: We really need to make sure that everything is looked after properly.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Anyway, let's make some notes to see just what needs doing to improve the Center.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now what about the computers?
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: I think it might be a good idea to install some new models.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: They would take up a lot less room, and so that would increase the workspace for textbooks and so on.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: That would be great.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: It is a bit cramped in there at times.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: What about other resources?
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Do you have a list of things that the students would like to see improved?
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Yes, one of the comments that students frequently make is that they find it difficult to find materials that are appropriate for their level.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: Especially reading resources, so I think we need to label them more clearly.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: Well that's easy enough, we can get that organized very quickly.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Hmm, in fact, I think we should review all of the study resources, as some of them are looking a bit out of date.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Definitely.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: The CD section especially needs to be more current.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: I think we should get some of the ones that go with our latest course books, and also make multiple copies.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Now, I was also thinking about some different materials that we haven't got in there at all.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: What do you think of the idea of introducing some workbooks?
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: If we break them up into separate pages and laminate them, they'd be a great resource.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: The students could study the main course book in class, and then do follow-up practice in the Self-access Center.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: That sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: OK, now finally we need to think about how the room is used.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: I'll have to talk to the teachers and make sure we can all reach some agreement on a timetable to supervise the Center after class.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: But we also need to think about security too, especially if we're going to invest in some new equipment.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: Um. What about putting in an alarm?
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: Good idea.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: The other thing I'd like to do is talk to our technicians and see whether we could somehow limit the access to email.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: I really don't want to see that resource misused.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: What about if we agree to only use it before and after class?
audioscript.speaker: JUN

audioscript.text: Yes, that would be fine.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: OK, anyway, that's great for now.
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: We'll discuss it further when we've managed to...
audioscript.speaker: PAM

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Company Culture Types and Their Characteristics

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear the beginning of a lecture about business cultures.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now whether you're going to university to study business or some other subject, many of you will eventually end up working for a company of some kind.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now when you first start working somewhere, you will realize that the organization you've joined has certain characteristics. And we often refer to these social characteristics as the culture of the organization.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This includes its unwritten ideas, beliefs, values, and things like that.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One well-known writer has classified company cultures by identifying four major types.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The first type is called the power culture.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it's usually found in small organizations.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's the type of culture that needs a central source of power to be effective, and because control is in the hands of just one or two people.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There aren't many rules or procedures.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another characteristic is that communication usually takes the form of conversations rather than say formal meetings or written memos.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now one of the benefits of this culture is that the organization has the ability to act quickly, so it responds well to threat or danger on the one hand, and opportunity on the other.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But on the negative side, this type of organization doesn't always act effectively, because it depends too much on one or two people at the top.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And when these people make poor decisions, there's no one else who can influence them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the kind of person who does well in this type of business culture is one who is happy to take risks, and for whom job security is a low priority.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The next type is known as role culture.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's r o l e, not r o double L by the way.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And this type is usually found in large companies.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which have lots of different levels in them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These organizations usually have separate departments that specialize in things like finance or sales or maintenance or whatever.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Each one is coordinated at the top by a small group of senior managers, and typically everyone's job is controlled by sets of rules and procedures.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, there are specific job descriptions, rules for discipline, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What are the benefits of this kind of culture?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well firstly, because it's found in large organizations, its fixed costs, or overheads as they're known,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: are low in relation to its output, or what it produces.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In other words, it can achieve economies of scale.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And secondly, it is particularly successful in business markets where technical expertise is important.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On the other hand, this culture is often very slow to recognize the need for change, and even slower to react.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What kind of person does this type of culture suit?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, it suits employees who value security, and who don't particularly want to have responsibility.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Moving on now to task cultures, this type is found in organizations that are project oriented.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You usually find it where the market for the company's product is extremely competitive.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or where the products themselves have a short lifespan.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Usually top management delegates the projects, the people, and other resources, and once these have been allocated, little day-to-day control is exercised from the top.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because this would seem like 'breaking the rules'.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now one of the major benefits of this culture is that it's flexible, but it does have some major disadvantages too.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For instance, it can't produce economies of scale or great depth of expertise.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: People who like working in groups or teams prefer this type of culture.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And finally the fourth category is called the Person Culture...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You usually find it where the market for the company's product is extremely competitive.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or where the products themselves have a short lifespan.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Usually top management delegates the projects, the people, and other resources, and once these have been allocated, little day-to-day control is exercised from the top.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because this would seem like 'breaking the rules'.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now one of the major benefits of this culture is that it's flexible, but it does have some major disadvantages too.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For instance, it can't produce economies of scale or great depth of expertise.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: People who like working in groups or teams prefer this type of culture.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And finally the fourth category is called the Person Culture...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: rules and procedure, e.g. job <input> and rules for discipline

items: slow to see when <input> is needed
